subject: freelancers
one_liner: Payroll, benefits, and HR for a company with exactly one employee, who is also the founder, the manager, and the entire org chart.
brand_voice_notes: Deadpan HR-and-payroll-platform gravitas pointed at a freelancer who is, legally, a whole company of one. Treats a solo operator like a workforce that needs onboarding, withholdings, a benefits administrator, and a performance review. Speaks fluent payroll: gross pay, FICA, year-to-date, open enrollment, approval chains, retention. Then applies all of it to a single person who would otherwise just transfer money to themselves. Never winks. The bit is that the product is sincerely, exhaustively correct, and completely unnecessary. Figures read like a real pay stub, set in tabular figures, not a console readout.

hero.h1: Real payroll for a company of one.
hero.sub: Headcount runs biweekly payroll from your checking account to your checking account, withholds the taxes, files them, and mails you a stub. You are the founder, the only employee, and as of today, the HR department. Onboarding takes four minutes.

features.title: Payroll that pays you
features.blurb: On the 1st and the 15th, Headcount pulls your pay from your account, withholds federal and state tax, files it, and deposits the rest back two business days later with a stub attached. Net pay, year-to-date totals, the works. The money makes a round trip. You have never been this employed.

features.title: Benefits for a workforce of one
features.blurb: Enroll yourself in a 401(k), then administer it. Set your own match and approve it. Open enrollment is a calendar event you scheduled, attended alone, and ran to completion. We file the paperwork with an agency that assumes there are more of you.

features.title: The org chart resolves to you
features.blurb: Request time off and approve it as your own manager. Submit three expenses and reject one to stay disciplined. Your annual review is written by your entire team, which is you, working late, being honest about you.

pricing_tiers.name: Sole Proprietor
pricing_tiers.tagline: For the freelancer who is technically a business and resents the implication.
perks: One employee on file, which is the maximum
Biweekly payroll to and from yourself
A pay stub mailed to your own address

pricing_tiers.name: Fully Staffed
pricing_tiers.tagline: For the operator running an entire company without ever delegating.
perks: An org chart with one node, reporting to itself
A year-end W-2 you mail to your own address

pricing_tiers.name: Enterprise of One
pricing_tiers.tagline: For the freelancer with a real headcount problem, which is that it is one.
perks: Unlimited departments, every seat filled by you
A performance-improvement plan you may accept or contest
A dedicated HR liaison contractually obligated to be you

testimonials.quote: I requested two weeks off and my manager approved it by end of day. My manager is me. I have never felt this supported at a job.
testimonials.name: Renata Cho
testimonials.title: Sole Proprietor and Acting Head of People, a Company of One

testimonials.quote: My first pay stub arrived in March and I cried at the kitchen table. The money left my account and came back smaller. That is what being employed feels like, and I had been missing it.
testimonials.name: Desmond Pryce
testimonials.title: Freelance Motion Designer and His Own Payroll Department

testimonials.quote: My annual review flagged me as a retention risk. I wrote the review. I approved the counteroffer. I stayed for the culture, which is also me.
testimonials.name: Lottie Vinstra
testimonials.title: Independent Brand Strategist, Founder, and Sole Member of the Talent Committee

demo_screen_concept: A payroll-and-HR admin console for a one-person company, titled 'Headcount HQ', with a left nav switching between People, Run Payroll, Time Off, and Reviews. People shows a team directory containing exactly one employee card (you, with title, start date, and a small org-chart node that loops back to report to itself) plus an 'Invite a teammate' button that opens a modal explaining the only seat is already filled. Run Payroll is a single-row payroll wizard: your gross pay, a live withholding breakdown (federal, state, Social Security, Medicare) in tabular figures, your net pay, and a 'Run payroll' button that animates the money leaving your account and returning two days later before generating a printable pay stub. Time Off lets you submit a PTO request that drops into an approvals queue as one pending item with an 'Approve as manager' button; approving it stamps it 'approved by you.' Reviews is a self-review: a slider where you rate your own performance, a 360-feedback panel sourced entirely from you, and a toggle to either place yourself on a performance-improvement plan or grant yourself a retention raise. Everything is mocked client-side.

design_direction.style: Mid-century editorial HR-corporate, warm-paper annual-report mode. A warm oat-cream ground (a faint printed-stock grain, generous margins, a thin rule framing the page like a 1960s corporate annual report) carries a structured editorial column grid. Headlines are set large and friendly in Calistoga, a warm rounded display with the confidence of a mid-century corporate wordmark; Hanken Grotesk carries calm humanist body copy and all interface labels. There is no console mono anywhere: every figure that pretends to be payroll (gross pay, withholdings, net pay, year-to-date totals, tier prices, match percentages) is set in Hanken Grotesk with tabular lining figures so it reads like a printed pay stub, not a terminal readout. One deep ink-blue carries the brand, the framing rules, headers, structural ink, and the single primary CTA; one rationed marigold is reserved strictly for the live 'run payroll' affordance, the active toggle, the small 'headcount: 1' stamp, and the marigold underline on the one link that matters, and appears almost nowhere else. Section dividers are a single hairline ink rule. Feature rows are laid out like report spreads: a numbered editorial column on the left, generous copy on the right. Motion is restrained and editorial: section headers ease up and fade in on scroll, the payroll round-trip animates calmly, the approve-as-manager stamp presses in, numbers settle. No gradients beyond a faint paper vignette, no glassmorphism, no neon, no hard offset shadows, no bento tiles, no skeuomorphic textures, no dark mode. Reads like a sober, well-funded payroll and HR company that has been pointed, with total seriousness, at a single freelancer who is legally a corporation and emotionally a staff of one.

fonts.body: 'Hanken Grotesk', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif

design_direction.archetype: Pricing-focused payroll/HR SaaS landing rendered as a mid-century corporate annual report: a warm-paper hero with a large Calistoga wordmark, a deadpan headline, and an 'org chart of one' plate showing a single employee node reporting to itself; three feature spreads with numbered editorial columns divided by hairline ink rules; three pull-quote testimonials with framed corporate headshots; a three-tier pricing rack styled as company plans with the middle tier marked as standard; a single ink-blue CTA; and a demo handoff to the Headcount HQ payroll-and-HR console.
design_direction.motion_vocab: Restrained and editorial: section headers translate up 12px and fade in on scroll with a soft ease-out, the payroll round-trip animates the money leaving and returning over ~900ms, the approve-as-manager stamp presses inward like a rubber stamp, tabular figures settle on a brief count-up, and the marigold link underline draws left-to-right on hover. Micro-interactions 150-280ms, ease-out entering, exits faster; all motion gated behind prefers-reduced-motion.
design_direction.hero_composition_notes: Split hero on the oat-cream ground inside a thin ink framing rule. Left column carries a large Calistoga 'Headcount' wordmark, a small 'a company of one' kicker, the deadpan h1, sub, the ink-blue 'Onboard yourself' CTA, and a quiet marigold-underlined 'open the demo' link. Right column is an editorial 'org chart of one' plate: a single framed employee node with a self-referential reporting line looping back to itself, a small marigold 'headcount: 1' stamp, and one stamped pay-stub figure (net pay, this period) set in tabular figures. A hairline ink rule closes the hero.

images.rationale: Typographic mid-century editorial annual-report hero, so the hero photo was skipped: the Calistoga wordmark and a CSS/SVG 'org chart of one' plate carry the fold, no imagery role. Demo subject assets were skipped because the demo is a payroll-and-HR admin console (dashboard/document-shaped, not photo-shaped). Full budget went to the always-applicable fallback: three deadpan corporate testimonial headshots of solo freelancers, each shot against a warm oat-cream backdrop with a deep ink-blue palette edge so they sit natively inside the report's framed quote blocks. Budget: 3 of 4 used, all on people; one slot left unused. All three passed vision judging on the first attempt.

items.prompt: Deadpan corporate headshot photograph of a composed East Asian woman in her early forties, a solo freelancer who runs her own one-person company, plain warm-toned blazer over a simple top, neutral self-possessed expression, looking directly at camera like a sincere staff-directory portrait. Softly out-of-focus warm oat-cream backdrop near #F3EAD6 with a faint hint of deep ink-blue #1F3B5C at one edge. Even soft daylight, mid-century editorial corporate-portrait mood, gently warm muted grade with a touch of marigold #D98A1F in the skin. Real photograph, one focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI.
items.judge: pass — composed direct-gaze portrait, deadpan, grey blazer over teal, oat-cream wall with an ink-blue band at the top edge that matches the palette, no text artifacts

items.prompt: Deadpan corporate headshot photograph of an ordinary Black man in his mid-thirties, a freelance motion designer who is his own payroll department, plain crew-neck sweater, calm slightly tired neutral expression, direct eye contact like a sincere company about-page portrait. Softly out-of-focus warm oat-cream backdrop near #F3EAD6 with a faint deep ink-blue #1F3B5C edge light. Even soft daylight, mid-century editorial corporate-portrait mood, warm muted grade. Real photograph, one focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI.
items.judge: pass — sincere about-page portrait, navy crew-neck harmonizes with the ink-blue, warm oat interior, distinct second subject, no artifacts

items.prompt: Deadpan corporate headshot photograph of a poised white woman in her late forties, an independent brand strategist and founder of her own company of one, short tidy hair, plain solid-color blouse, composed knowing closed-mouth expression, looking straight at camera like an enterprise leadership directory photo. Softly out-of-focus warm oat-cream backdrop near #F3EAD6 with a subtle deep ink-blue #1F3B5C edge. Even soft daylight, mid-century editorial corporate-portrait mood, warm muted grade with a faint marigold #D98A1F warmth. Real photograph, one focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI.
items.judge: pass — poised leadership-directory portrait, silver hair, grey top, oat-cream backdrop with an ink-blue band on the right edge, deadpan, on-palette, no artifacts
